text: The absorption mechanism of mercury ions (Hg(II)) in the human intestine has so far been studied by animal experiments and static cell culture. However, these models are far from the true physiological microenvironment of the intestine and are unable to provide real-time dynamic information about the intestinal absorption of heavy metals. Here, we developed an organ-on-a-chip gut model device with label-free sensors to dynamically mimic the physiological environment of the intestine and realize the intestinal absorption of Hg(II) in situ. Our gut-on-a-chip device with the added benefit of function can not only achieve the dynamic intestinal environment by applying the optimized fluid flow and cyclic stretching (0.02 dyne/cm2 and 1%, 0.15 Hz) but also mimic the shape of the intestinal villi and improve the degree of cellular differentiation. The added TEER and electrochemical sensors can dynamically monitor the integrity of the intestinal barrier and Hg(II) transport. Our experiments showed that the intestinal barrier integrity and the intestinal physiological environment simulated by the fluid flow and cyclic stretching can improve the formation of intestinal villi and promote the degree of cellular differentiation, which were significantly different from those in static cell culture. The exposure of Hg(II) ions to the intestinal barrier will lead to physical destruction of the epithelial barrier, which leads to a significant decrease in TEER and a decrease in the expression of ZO-1 and Ezrin proteins. By changing the tensile strain from 1% to 5%, the intestinal absorption of Hg(II) increased by 23.59% and was significantly correlated with the upregulation of Piezo1 and DMT1.
